frameworks.name: common drive (single-driver MU recruitment)
frameworks.one_line: cortex supplies one shared drive to each motor-unit pool

frameworks.name: Henneman size principle
frameworks.one_line: motor units recruited in fixed order by size

frameworks.name: multiple-drives / flexible MU control
frameworks.one_line: behaviour-dependent, multi-dimensional cortical control of motor units

frameworks.name: high-dimensional motor cortex population coding
frameworks.one_line: M1 population activity spans many degrees of freedom enabling flexible MU control

core_question: Are motor units controlled by a single common drive following a rigid recruitment order, or can cortex flexibly control them in a behaviour-dependent way?
key_assumptions: many MUs can be recorded simultaneously across varied force profiles
an isometric task can stabilize MU identity during rapid force changes
Neuropixels-scale cortical sampling reflects M1 control signals
manipulations: force profile (diverse and rapidly changing isometric forces)
behavioural task context driving MU activity
simultaneous recording site within motor cortex
key_findings: MU activity patterns were behaviour-dependent rather than fixed
MU activity could not be captured by a single common drive but required multiple drives
motor cortex population responses had many degrees of freedom
neighboring cortical sites recruited different MUs
interpretations: MUs are flexibly controlled to meet task demands, violating strict common-drive and rigid size-principle accounts
M1 population structure provides the dimensionality needed for flexible, near-independent MU control
